text: The robotic surgical system is used to perform fenestration on a splenic cyst, with robotic arms maneuvering instruments to access and open the cyst.

The patient is a fifty year old female with chronic left upper quadrant pain and postprandial fullness. She had a history of asthma and morbid obesity. A CT abdomen pelvis was obtained for her preoperative workup showing a thirteen centimeter splenic cyst with benign features.
The CT scan axial cuts display an 11 by 13 centimeter splenic cyst with benign features, while the coronal cuts reveal the cyst causing displacement of the normal splenic parenchyma.
Due to her persistent symptoms, robotic splenic cyst fenestration was recommended.

Four 8mm robotic trocars are placed across the upper abdomen. Laparoscopic scissors are used to open the splenic cyst, and approximately 850 cc's of brown fluid are aspirated. The robotic system is then docked.
The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions, addressing dense adhesions between the abdominal wall, liver, and omentum using robotic instruments.

Endocyanine green dye is administered to visually delineate the interface between the normal splenic parenchyma and the cyst capsule. The surgeon marks the margin with a monopolar hook and uses a vessel sealer to divide the anterior cyst wall from the splenic parenchyma.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) dye to identify the border between the splenic capsule and spleen at the superior medial aspect. The final attachments are divided using a vessel sealer, completing the separation of the cyst from the splenic parenchyma.
The surgeon irrigates the cyst cavity and evaluates it for hemostasis. A bleeding vessel is identified and treated using a bipolar device. The cavity is irrigated again to ensure hemostasis.

An omental patch is positioned to fill the cyst cavity, and a 19 French Blake drain is placed. One of the 8mm trocars is removed, and the site is enlarged to facilitate the placement of a laparoscopic retrieval bag, which is used to remove the specimen.
Postoperatively, the patient was started on a regular diet. The drain was removed on postoperative day three and the patient was discharged home.

Final pathology returned as dense fibrous tissue consistent with benign splenic pseudocyst. At the time of follow-up, her left upper quadrant pain and postprandial fullness had significantly improved.
